content.problemStatement:
We make exactly $2N - 2$ moves, $N - 1$ are "right" moves and the other $N - 1$ are "down" moves. \
We want to align these moves such that there are exactly $K$ "turn" cells, meaning - we reach the cell with a move different than we leave the cell.

If we fix the first move to "right", in how many combinations can we reach the last cell.

1. $$
^{N - 2}C_{\lfloor\frac{K}{2}\rfloor} \cdot ^{N - 2}C_{\lfloor\frac{K - 1}{2}\rfloor}
$$

2. $$
^{N - 1}C_{\lfloor\frac{K}{2}\rfloor} \cdot ^{N - 1}C_{\lfloor\frac{K - 1}{2}\rfloor}
$$

3. $$
^{N}C_{\lfloor\frac{K}{2}\rfloor} \cdot ^{N}C_{\lfloor\frac{K - 1}{2}\rfloor}
$$

4. None of these
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
